description:
You are an integration developer who keeps a small collection of JSON
    request/response stubs under

        /home/user/api_samples/

    (the directory and its contents already exist).

    1) Create a gzip-compressed tar archive of that directory named

        /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz

    2) Immediately extract the newly-created archive into a **new**
       directory

        /home/user/restore_test/

       so that, after extraction, the following files are present:

        /home/user/restore_test/sample1.json
        /home/user/restore_test/sample2.json
        /home/user/restore_test/sample3.json

       and every file’s byte-for-byte content matches the original file in
       /home/user/api_samples/.

    3) Append exactly two log lines to the text file

        /home/user/backup.log

       using **precisely** the format shown below (the automated checker
       does a literal comparison, so any deviation will fail the test):

            2024-01-15T00:00:00 | ARCHIVE_CREATED   | /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz
            2024-01-15T00:00:05 | ARCHIVE_EXTRACTED | /home/user/restore_test/

       • Use tabs or spaces exactly as in the template  
       • Keep the timestamps exactly as given (they are
         intentionally fixed for reproducible testing)

    Summary of required final artefacts:

      a) /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz    → gzip tar archive of api_samples
      b) /home/user/restore_test/…       → extracted copy of the three JSON files
      c) /home/user/backup.log           → contains the two log lines above,
                                           appended if the file already exists

    No other output is required. The whole job can be done with three to four
    standard shell commands (e.g., tar, mkdir). You do **not** need root
    privileges. Make sure that the exact paths, file names, and log-file
    contents match what is specified here so the automated tests can verify
    your work.
truth:
Initial state (already present when the task starts):

      Directory: /home/user/api_samples/

        sample1.json
            {
              "id": 1,
              "status": "ok",
              "data": "alpha"
            }

        sample2.json
            {
              "id": 2,
              "status": "ok",
              "data": "beta"
            }

        sample3.json
            {
              "id": 3,
              "status": "ok",
              "data": "gamma"
            }

    All files have Unix line endings and a trailing newline.

    Expected post-task state:

      1) File /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip
         tarball containing exactly one top-level directory 'api_samples'
         with the three JSON files above; internal timestamps and order are
         irrelevant.

      2) Directory /home/user/restore_test/ exists and contains

            /home/user/restore_test/api_samples  (auto-generated by tar -x)
                    ├── sample1.json (content identical to source)
                    ├── sample2.json (identical)
                    └── sample3.json (identical)

         Note: some tar versions may strip the top-level directory on
         extraction; the tests will accept either of these two layouts:

            /home/user/restore_test/api_samples/sample1.json
         or
            /home/user/restore_test/sample1.json

      3) File /home/user/backup.log ends with exactly these two new lines
         (in this order, each terminated by a single '\n'):

            2024-01-15T00:00:00 | ARCHIVE_CREATED   | /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz
            2024-01-15T00:00:05 | ARCHIVE_EXTRACTED | /home/user/restore_test/

    The automated checker will:

      • Verify that /home/user/api_backup.tar.gz exists and is a gzip file
        whose extracted payload matches byte-for-byte the three JSON source
        files.

      • Verify that /home/user/restore_test/ contains an exact copy of those
        files (either directly or inside a top-level api_samples directory).

      • Read /home/user/backup.log and confirm that the last two lines are
        identical to the required fixed-timestamp lines above (nothing more,
        nothing less).

    No additional files, directories, or log lines are permitted.
